name: Biomolecules
subject: Chemistry

sections.heading: Carbohydrates — Classification
sections.body:
Carbohydrates are optically active polyhydroxy aldehydes or ketones, or compounds that yield such units on hydrolysis. General formula Cx(H2O)y (with exceptions like rhamnose).

Classification by hydrolysis:

  • Monosaccharides: cannot be hydrolysed further. Examples: glucose, fructose, galactose, ribose. Sub-classified as aldoses (CHO) or ketoses (C=O); and by C count: triose (C3), tetrose (C4), pentose (C5), hexose (C6).
  • Disaccharides: yield 2 monosaccharide units on hydrolysis. Sucrose → glucose + fructose; maltose → 2 glucose; lactose → glucose + galactose.
  • Polysaccharides: yield many monosaccharide units. Starch, cellulose, glycogen.

By taste/reducibility: sugars (sweet, soluble, crystalline) vs non-sugars (starch, cellulose). Reducing sugars reduce Tollen's/Fehling's (free –CHO or hemiacetal –OH on C1): all monosaccharides, maltose, lactose. Non-reducing: sucrose (both anomeric carbons engaged in glycosidic bond).

sections.heading: Glucose — Structure, Open and Cyclic Forms
sections.body:
Glucose (C6H12O6): aldohexose. Open-chain Fischer projection: CHO–CHOH–CHOH–CHOH–CHOH–CH2OH with all –OH on the right except C3 (D-glucose).

Evidence for open-chain structure: forms cyanohydrin with HCN (–CHO present); reduces Tollen's/Fehling's; oxidation with Br2/H2O gives gluconic acid (mild oxidation of –CHO); HNO3 oxidation gives saccharic (glucaric) acid (oxidises both –CHO and terminal –CH2OH); reduction with Na/Hg gives sorbitol (1° alcohol from CHO).

Some reactions don't fit the open-chain form: glucose does not react with NaHSO3 or Schiff's reagent; only one of the five OH groups is replaced by –OCH3 when treated with CH3OH/HCl; mutarotation observed.

These are explained by the cyclic hemiacetal structures: C5–OH attacks C1 –CHO to form a 6-membered pyranose ring. The new chiral centre at C1 gives two anomers: α-D-glucose (–OH at C1 down) and β-D-glucose (–OH at C1 up). In aqueous solution they interconvert via the open form (mutarotation): solution equilibrates at specific rotation +52.5°.

Haworth projection: 6-membered pyranose ring with O between C1 and C5; substituents are above/below the ring plane.

sections.heading: Fructose, Disaccharides, Polysaccharides
sections.body:
Fructose: ketohexose; in cyclic form is a 5-membered furanose ring (O between C2 and C5). Found in honey, fruits. Despite being a ketose, fructose reduces Tollen's and Fehling's because in alkaline medium it isomerises (via enediol) to glucose/mannose.

Sucrose: α-D-glucopyranose + β-D-fructofuranose linked by α-1,β-2 glycosidic bond. Both anomeric C atoms are involved → no free hemiacetal → non-reducing. Hydrolysis (dilute acid or invertase enzyme) gives equimolar glucose + fructose. The mixture is called invert sugar because optical rotation flips sign (sucrose +66.5° → invert sugar net −20°).

Maltose: α-D-glucopyranose + α-D-glucopyranose, α-1,4-glycosidic. Free anomeric C on second unit → reducing.

Lactose: β-D-galactopyranose + β-D-glucopyranose, β-1,4-glycosidic. Reducing.

Polysaccharides:

  • Starch: storage in plants. Made of amylose (linear, α-1,4-linked, 15–20%) and amylopectin (branched, α-1,4 + α-1,6 at branches, 80–85%).
  • Cellulose: structural polymer in plant cell walls; β-1,4-linked glucose units → straight chains held by H-bonds; humans cannot digest (no enzyme for β-1,4); ruminants can.
  • Glycogen: "animal starch"; like amylopectin but more branched; stored in liver and muscle.

sections.heading: Proteins — Amino Acids and Structure
sections.body:
α-Amino acids: H2N–CHR–COOH. About 20 standard ones in proteins. They exist as zwitterions ⁺H3N–CHR–COO⁻ in solid and aqueous solution → high m.p., dipolar; soluble in water.

At a specific pH (the isoelectric point pI) the amino acid is electrically neutral. By –R group:

  • Neutral: glycine, alanine, valine, leucine.
  • Acidic: glutamic acid, aspartic acid (extra –COOH).
  • Basic: lysine, arginine (extra –NH2).

Essential: cannot be synthesised by the body, must come from diet (e.g. valine, leucine, lysine, methionine, phenylalanine, threonine, tryptophan, isoleucine; histidine, arginine for children). Non-essential: synthesised by body (e.g. glycine, alanine, serine, glutamic acid).

Peptide bond: –CO–NH– formed by condensation of –COOH of one amino acid with –NH2 of another. Two amino acids → dipeptide; many → polypeptide → protein (typically > 50 amino acid residues).

Levels of structure:

  1. Primary: linear sequence of amino acids in the polypeptide chain.
  2. Secondary: local folding stabilised by H-bonds between C=O and N–H of backbone — α-helix (right-handed, 3.6 residues per turn) and β-pleated sheet.
  3. Tertiary: 3D folding of the entire chain, stabilised by H-bonds, disulphide (–S–S–) bridges, ionic, and hydrophobic interactions.
  4. Quaternary: aggregation of multiple polypeptide subunits (e.g. haemoglobin, four subunits).

Fibrous proteins (keratin in hair, myosin in muscle): long parallel chains, water-insoluble, structural.
Globular proteins (albumin, insulin, enzymes): coiled to roughly spherical shape, water-soluble.

Denaturation: loss of secondary, tertiary, quaternary structure (with retention of primary structure) by heat, acid/base, organic solvents, heavy metals. Examples: cooking egg (albumin), curdling of milk. Globular proteins coagulate to fibrous form, losing biological activity.

sections.heading: Enzymes
sections.body:
Enzymes are biocatalysts — almost all are globular proteins (some are RNA, ribozymes). They are highly specific (one substrate or one type of reaction), efficient (10⁶–10¹² rate enhancement), and operate at mild T (≈ 37 °C) and pH (≈ 7).

Mechanism: substrate binds to a specific active site on the enzyme (lock-and-key or induced-fit model), enzyme–substrate complex forms, reaction occurs, product is released; enzyme is regenerated.

Many enzymes need cofactors: metal ions or organic molecules (vitamins acting as coenzymes). Activity destroyed by denaturation. Examples: amylase (starch → maltose), pepsin (proteins → smaller peptides in stomach), trypsin (polypeptides → amino acids in intestine), urease (urea → ammonia + CO2).

sections.heading: Vitamins
sections.body:
Vitamins: organic compounds required in small amounts for normal growth and metabolism; cannot be synthesised by the body in adequate amounts (with exceptions like vitamin D). Classified by solubility:

  • Fat-soluble: A, D, E, K. Stored in liver and adipose tissue. Excess A or D is toxic.
  • Water-soluble: B group (B1 thiamine, B2 riboflavin, B6 pyridoxine, B12 cobalamin, niacin, pantothenic acid, biotin, folic acid) and C (ascorbic acid). Excretion via urine; need regular intake. Vitamin B12 stored in liver.

Selected deficiency diseases: A → night blindness, xerophthalmia; B1 → beri-beri; B2 → cheilosis; B3 (niacin) → pellagra; B6 → convulsions; B12 → pernicious anaemia; C → scurvy; D → rickets, osteomalacia; E → reproductive issues / muscular weakness; K → impaired blood clotting.

sections.heading: Nucleic Acids — DNA and RNA
sections.body:
Nucleic acids store and transmit genetic information. Two types: DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) and RNA (ribonucleic acid).

Building blocks (in order of complexity):

  • Nucleoside = base + sugar (ribose or 2-deoxyribose).
  • Nucleotide = base + sugar + phosphate. Linked into a polynucleotide chain by 3' → 5' phosphodiester bonds.

Bases:

  • Purines: Adenine (A), Guanine (G) — both in DNA and RNA.
  • Pyrimidines: Cytosine (C) in both; Thymine (T) only in DNA; Uracil (U) only in RNA.

Structure of DNA (Watson–Crick): double helix; two right-handed antiparallel strands wound around a common axis. Sugar = 2-deoxyribose. Strands held by H-bonds between complementary bases: A=T (2 H-bonds) and G≡C (3 H-bonds). Chargaff's rule: A% = T%, G% = C%.

RNA: single-stranded; sugar = ribose; T replaced by U. Three main types:

  • mRNA (messenger): carries genetic code from DNA to ribosome.
  • tRNA (transfer): brings specific amino acids to the ribosome during protein synthesis.
  • rRNA (ribosomal): structural and catalytic component of ribosomes.

Biological functions: DNA stores hereditary information; replication ensures faithful transmission to daughter cells. Protein synthesis = transcription (DNA → mRNA) + translation (mRNA → protein, via tRNA at the ribosome, reading triplet codons).

summary: Biomolecules are the molecular machinery of life. Carbohydrates are polyhydroxy aldehydes/ketones, classified as mono- (glucose, fructose), di- (sucrose, maltose, lactose), and polysaccharides (starch = amylose + amylopectin, cellulose, glycogen). Glucose exists as α/β pyranose anomers in equilibrium (mutarotation) via open-chain CHO. Sucrose is non-reducing; its hydrolysis gives invert sugar. Proteins are condensation polymers of α-amino acids (zwitterions, with isoelectric pI); peptide bonds (–CO–NH–) link them; structure has primary (sequence), secondary (α-helix, β-sheet, H-bonds), tertiary (3D fold, S–S, hydrophobic), and quaternary levels. Denaturation destroys higher structure but keeps the primary sequence. Enzymes are highly specific globular protein catalysts. Vitamins are essential micronutrients: fat-soluble (A, D, E, K) vs water-soluble (B group, C). Nucleic acids: nucleotides (base + sugar + phosphate) form DNA (double helix, deoxyribose, A=T, G≡C) and RNA (single-stranded, ribose, U replaces T) and direct heredity and protein synthesis.
pitfalls: Calling sucrose a reducing sugar — it isn't; both anomeric C atoms are tied up in the glycosidic bond.
Forgetting fructose (a ketose) reduces Tollen's/Fehling's because of base-catalysed isomerisation.
Mixing up amylose (linear, α-1,4) and amylopectin (branched, α-1,4 + α-1,6).
Saying denaturation destroys the primary structure — it doesn't.
Pairing the wrong DNA bases — A pairs with T (2 H-bonds), G with C (3 H-bonds).
Saying RNA contains thymine — it has uracil instead.
Forgetting that humans can't digest cellulose (no β-1,4 glucosidase).
Confusing essential (must be eaten) with non-essential (body makes them) amino acids.
